words: This fourth interview with Clara Payne Grove took place at her home in Moscow,
Idaho, on December 16, 1975. The interviewer Is Sam Schrager.
CLARA PAYNE GROVE: — Moscow. I do know that I had a pickup at my door, well,
I suppose at least 20 years ago, and I think it's more than
that. And that's the first I knew about it, and I never thought of
it again until they were looking for a person for the position that I
have, a representative, it used to be called the Center, and then they
got high -toned a little more and began to call us representatives. I
never knew why they said Center, because you're no more the center of
anything than anybody else in it is. But that's what I was for eleven
years, til they changed it. But, it's really an international organi
zation. They have it various other countries. I don't know just how
many. And, I'm proud of Moscow. I don't know whether they tell every
representative that or not, maybe they don't, but they say that Moscow
turns in more goods than any place in the area. And the area goes from
Moses Lake to Missoula from Walla Walla to the boundary, so it's a pret
ty good sized area. But I did a lot of work on it before I got it work
ed up. I would stand on the street and hand out cards. I'd stop peo
ple and tell them about it. Every week I would call ten on the phone
and advertise it. And. now, I am not advertising because we are getting
all the goods that the big truck will take away. Well, there's no use
of us getting more than they'll take away, so I'm not advertising
We do have this slump right now before Christmas, but people
don't want to be called on an advertisement right now, they're too busy!
So, they get a rest from me.
SAM SCHRAGER: I wanted to ask you about some of the attitudes in the early days that
you remember, the way people felt about things because it's pretty clear
to me that things have changed quite a bit in the general thinking of
people in these days. And one thing I wanted to ask you about was the
role of women, because I have this idea in the early days, it was more
along the lines of a — the husband ruled and the wife obeyed. Now is
an accurate idea of what it was like then?
CLARA PAYNE GROVE: Well, it used to be that there was a very distinct line be
tween the sexes. And, while the line is still there, everybody is—
well, not everybody— but most of the people are doing their best to
do anything they can to obscure the line; to erase it. Well, they
can t erase it, but they^liave pretty well obscured it. But, it used
to be that a woman's place was defined. Well, it isn't now. Her place
is any yahoo thing that she wants to do or work at or play with, or
anything and it's accepted. And those ridiculous pantsuits are more
than accepted; they're fairly worshipped! And, at the time I was
young, you never heard of such a thing as a woman wearing trousers!
In a great emergency, when she was compelled to do it, she would put
on her husband's— well, I was going to say Levis— I'll tell you how
I got reproved for saying that—but she would put on her husband's
overalls over her dress for whatever she had to do, like cleaning the
barn or so on. But she didn't wear it after she went back to the house,
it came off when she went in. And there was no such thing as a pantsuit—
which, well, I have my own idea of them!

speaker: SS
words: If you could describe how a woman's role was defined; how her life was

speaker: SS
words: defined in those days—how would it be?

speaker: CG
words: Well, we did the housework, and I look at it from a farm viewpoint be

speaker: CG
words: cause I was born and raised on a farm; and I'm a farmer yet. But if

speaker: CG
words: they were on a farm, the woman took care of the chickens almost entirely. It was seldom

speaker: CG
words: that a man took care of the chickens. That was

speaker: CG
words: woman's work. And, more especially in the people of German extraction,

speaker: CG
words: the women would carry the swill pail to the hogs. Maybe you don't know

speaker: CG
words: what a swill pail is! Well, every kitchen had a swill pail, and that's

speaker: CG
words: the name it went by, and into it went— well, we would call it garbage,

speaker: CG
words: but into that went liquid leavings, like the glass of milk that had been

speaker: CG
words: partly drunk, or the coffee that was left in the cup or the coffeepot.

speaker: CG
words: ^And, if water was scarce, the dishwater always went into the swill pail

speaker: CG
words: and sometimes even if water was not scarce. It was quite common to em

speaker: CG
words: pty the dishpan, when you were through with the dishes, into the swill

speaker: CG
words: pail. And, in a great many homes a woman carried that and put it in

speaker: CG
words: the trough for the hogs. And, she did quite a lot of the garden work.

speaker: CG
words: In some cases, in many cases, she did all of the garden work; and every

speaker: CG
words: farm had a garden, of course. And, as now, it was quite common that

speaker: CG
words: she led the family devotions. And, she, in many families, ruled the

speaker: CG
words: children more, perhaps, than is common now. Although, I don't know,

speaker: CG
words: perhaps the fathers still don't pay much attention to the children. Leave

speaker: CG
words: them to the wife and the winds to grow up as best they can! And, she

speaker: CG
words: belonged to the circle of her church, and there were years that it went

speaker: CG
words: by the name of The Ladies Aid. And, they didn't divide it up into bun

speaker: CG
words: ches, it was all the women of the church. And, she taught school, most

speaker: CG
words: ly, and there were a few piano teachers. And, in general, they were

speaker: CG
words: the head of the children before the children were mature. And after

speaker: CG
words: they were mature, they got acquainted with their father more. And, as

speaker: CG
words: nearly as I can tell, women were more content to just be a housewife

speaker: CG
words: than they are now. I think they had a real contentment in their role.

speaker: CG
words: And they really looked to the men more for opinion and counseling and

speaker: CG
words: that sort of thing, although there has always been— this is one that I

speaker: CG
words: read and it was good. A woman was entertaining a friend, and the

speaker: CG
words: hostess' husband came stamping in and told the two women what he thought

speaker: CG
words: of the President and the Congress and the weather and one thing and an

speaker: CG
words: other, until he had finished what he had to say, he said, "I am the head

speaker: CG
words: of this household, and my wife knows it." And he stamped out. And when

speaker: CG
words: the sound of his footsteps had died away, his wife said, "Yes, he is the

speaker: CG
words: head of this house and I know it, but I'm the neck that turns the head,

speaker: CG
words: and he doesn't know it!" (Laughter) Well, that sort of defined a woman's

speaker: CG
words: position at that time. The husband was the head, but quite a lot of the

speaker: CG
words: women were the neck. Does that sort of define it?

speaker: SS
words: That certainly does— goes a long ways towards defining it. It's a big

speaker: SS
words: question, because it involves the whole society. Do you think that wom

speaker: SS
words: en in farms— rural areas had a much different kind of role than the

speaker: SS
words: women in towns? Of, course, I realize that on farms they had to do farm

speaker: SS
words: tasks, but do you think there was a very great difference between ?

speaker: CG
words: I think there was quite a difference, mostly. There would be exceptions,

speaker: CG
words: perhaps. But, I think mostly you knew which was the farm woman and

speaker: CG
words: which was the town woman. I think their lives were so different. The

speaker: CG
words: automobile coming in had a big effect on that matter. If a woman had

speaker: CG
words: to go and harness the farm team and hitch them to the lumberwagon, a

speaker: CG
words: buggy, if they had one—but everybody didn't—but she had to harness

speaker: CG
words: the team and hitch them to a vehicle and drive at a rate of six or eight

speaker: CG
words: miles an hour, she wasn't going to do a lot of going. In fact, she was

speaker: CG
words: n't going to do much of any going except on Saturday, when the father,--

speaker: CG
words: and Dad used to be Father or Pa, but there was no Dad— either he would

speaker: CG
words: go to town, or she would go to town or the whole family would go. And,

speaker: CG
words: there was this difference— when a mother went anywhere, of course, she

speaker: CG
words: took the children with her. What else could she do? There was no such

speaker: CG
words: thing as a babysitter. And, nobody felt the need of it. And it is true

speaker: CG
words: I don't think the children at that time were so much better, really, al

speaker: CG
words: though we didn't have this crime wave or anything similar to it in any

speaker: CG
words: way—but I don't mean to say that they were b£tter, but they were dif

speaker: CG
words: ferent. That is to say, if the mother took three or four young child

speaker: CG
words: ren with her, she would sit them each on a chair at an age when their

speaker: CG
words: feet would stick straight out before them, and they would sit there

speaker: CG
words: and they might possibly exchange words with each other once in a while,

speaker: CG
words: but they didn't interrupt conversations, and they were not noisy, and

speaker: CG
words: they didn't ask for anything to play with and they sat there. That was

speaker: CG
words: it. And they might sit there for a couple of hours without ever getting

speaker: CG
words: down or being among those present at all! So that people at that time

speaker: CG
words: were not like the people at this time. The adults had to be different

speaker: CG
words: or they could not have produced that difference in the children.

speaker: SS
words: Was it a demanding— an asserting of authority— demanding that the kids

speaker: SS
words: respect the authority of the parents and the rules and be obedient—

speaker: SS
words: just unquestioning?

speaker: CG
words: i think that the difference in the feminine and mascule authority over

speaker: CG
words: the children was just about the same as it is now. That is to say, —

speaker: CG
words: I wonder if I can express this just like I want it— the mother has a

speaker: CG
words: field of authority in which the children recognize it and are obedient.

speaker: CG
words: And the father has more of an overreach—the mother does not reach into

speaker: CG
words: the father's authority; but the father's authority does overreach the

speaker: CG
words: household. It's like this: A friend of mine between here and Troy had

speaker: CG
words: taken me out to visit her one day and she was going to bring me home,

speaker: CG
words: and the grandchildren had come from Troy to Grandma's for a visit, and

speaker: CG
words: she was going to bring the children in the car with her she brought

speaker: CG
words: me home. Well, the children got into the car and they started a regu

speaker: CG
words: lar rumpus royal, and Grandma told them they would have to be quiet beGROVE

speaker: CG
words: fore she could start the car. And, Grandma asked them to be quiet, and

speaker: CG
words: Grandma begged them to be quiet and she urged them to be quiet, and the

speaker: CG
words: riot went right on] And, Dad stepped up to the car and he just simply

speaker: CG
words: looked in at them for a little bit until they got quiet, which they did

speaker: CG
words: when Dad looked in there! And he simply said, quietly,"No fighting in

speaker: CG
words: the car." and walked away. And those children didn't put on another bit

speaker: CG
words: of rumpus the whole way to town. (Chuckles) that is the difference

speaker: CG
words: between mascule and feminine authority over children. I don't know

speaker: CG
words: well, yes, I do know why it is. The feminine and the masculine were

speaker: CG
words: created in that mold. And when you come right down to it, it isn't a

speaker: CG
words: bad mold.

speaker: SS
words: It means that the children have a certain amount of fear of their fath

speaker: SS
words: er, which they really don't have for their mother? Am I correct in say

speaker: SS
words: ing that?

speaker: CG
words: They don't feel toward the mother— I don't mean toward the mother— I

speaker: CG
words: mean the mother's authority— they don't feel toward it as they do to

speaker: CG
words: ward father's authority. It can be that there is a mother who is in

speaker: CG
words: the habit of whipping her children to get obedience, and she doesn't get

speaker: CG
words: it as father gets it. And perhaps he is always just simply quiet and

speaker: CG
words: mild with the children. But the male and the female were created as

speaker: CG
words: they were created, and we can't get around it! We can try, but we don't

speaker: CG
words: succeed.

speaker: SS
words: Could the mother on a farm— how would you consider her responsibility

speaker: SS
words: as compared to the father's? I mean to make decisions and that kind of

speaker: SS
words: thing? Her power to make decisions, as compared to the father's? Did

speaker: SS
words: she have to consult with him on any matter of importance?

speaker: CG
words: Well, depending on what it was. There were areas of strictly feminine

speaker: CG
words: work like cooking and so on, that she just ad-libbed as she pleased,

speaker: CG
words: and she was supposed to. But, if it came to any business matter then

speaker: CG
words: father's word was, unless she could persuade him, which some of us

speaker: CG
words: are pretty good at doing sometimes. But, the father must be consulted

speaker: CG
words: on all business, and if there was some thing that a child f doing that

speaker: CG
words: upsetting to the household, and wouldn t stop it for the mother, she

speaker: CG
words: relayed it to the father and let him deal with it, so that they recog

speaker: CG
words: nized the father's authority in a way they didn't the mother's, and yet

speaker: CG
words: there were many things that the mother's authority was first. Intimate

speaker: CG
words: things about— especially with little children. Although it was and

speaker: CG
words: still is a fact that— take a young baby that is very fretful and is

speaker: CG
words: crying and fussing and the mother can't quiet it, if the father takes

speaker: CG
words: it frequently the child quiets at once. Well, for one thing, some wom

speaker: CG
words: en say they are as strong as a man; they are not, it doesn't come out

speaker: CG
words: that way in a test. And, ( child doesn't feel any give as though

speaker: CG
words: it might, you know, slip out from. mother's arm, with father it

speaker: CG
words: doesn't feel that give, it feels the muscle that's under it, and that

speaker: CG
words: of itself is quieting. And, as a usual thing, the father is longer armed

speaker: CG
words: and broader shouldered and makes a more comfortable resting place for

speaker: CG
words: the child, more the size that it needs. So that both father and mother

speaker: CG
words: have a place and neither one is greater than that of the other. It's

speaker: CG
words: different but not greater.

speaker: SS
words: When you express the feeling that women were more contented in the role

speaker: SS
words: of homemaker than they are now— I find that interesting. Because it

speaker: SS
words: seems as though women had less authority than they have now. It seems

speaker: SS
words: as though, if the men made the decisions. I think in today's modern

speaker: SS
words: marriage decisions are looked at as being a matter of mutual consent,

speaker: SS
words: perhaps moreso than it used to be.

speaker: CG
words: Well, that is very personal to each. It isn't anything a person can

speaker: CG
words: generalize, and there are women at the present time who do have the

speaker: CG
words: old-time sense, and there are men who do have the old-time sense, but

speaker: CG
words: they are not so common. And, in my opinion, — on the radio I heard

speaker: CG
words: this scientist explain that the chemical effect of the chlorine that

speaker: CG
words: was ordered inAall drinking water, was ordered there by the Communists

speaker: CG
words: because its chemical effect is to reduce responsibility. And,Athe ir

speaker: CG
words: responsibility of the people at the present time is a shocking thing.

speaker: CG
words: So, I think perhaps that scientist might have been right that chlorine

speaker: CG
words: reduces responsibility.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that the amount of responsibility that the women had in

speaker: SS
words: those days was fair, was just? Or do you think that its been better to

speaker: SS
words: widen the rights and opportunities for women to go outside of the home

speaker: SS
words: and make their way in any way they can?

speaker: CG
words: Well, I think that that was about the same from the time of Adam and

speaker: CG
words: Eve until today. I doubt if it has varied greatly. While it's true

speaker: CG
words: that we have this women's lib and other foolishness now, but still down

speaker: CG
words: at the foundation of the matter, I think the ratio is perhaps somewhat

speaker: CG
words: the same. People are people, and they are different in different

speaker: CG
words: or to put it more correctly, they behave differently in different

speaker: CG
words: For instance; when I was a child, if I had addressed you as Sam, I would

speaker: CG
words: have been in high disgrace. Adults were Mr. and Mrs. and among the

speaker: CG
words: adults they were Mr. and Mrs. unless they were extremely intimate friends

speaker: CG
words: or related. We had none of this first name business. It just wasn't

speaker: CG
words: done.

speaker: SS
words: Did that mean that the friendships themselves were different in those

speaker: SS
words: days?

speaker: CG
words: Well, they — the behavior was different. And, in my opinion, the lack

speaker: CG
words: of formality that we have now »was promoted to promote the sex situation

speaker: CG
words: that prevails at the present time. And, I think everybody admits that

speaker: CG
words: it does prevail. The formality of our address; the fact that no such

speaker: CG
words: thing as male and female going down the street holding hands or with

speaker: CG
words: their arms around each other— there was no such thing seen or heard

speaker: CG
words: of— it just wasn't known. You couldn't do that. And I think that

speaker: CG
words: formality is a check on loose sexuality.

speaker: SS
words: This makes me think of a question that I have from when we talked be

speaker: SS
words: fore about divorce and about why divorce was considered so wrong in those

speaker: SS
words: days. Nowdays, of course, it's common as cake, it's not unusual at all.

speaker: CG
words: Well, we have the scripture for it that there is only one reason for

speaker: CG
words: divorce; just one, no more. And the people were closer to following

speaker: CG
words: the scriptural mandates than they are now. That would be one reason.

speaker: CG
words: And another reason— men and women were more content at that time than

speaker: CG
words: they are now. They were not up in the air all the time. They were—

speaker: CG
words: they either had or didn't have- whichever it was- and they were content

speaker: CG
words: with it. I'm old-time enough that in a prayer of thanksgiving I al

speaker: CG
words: ways say, that I have everything. Well, some people say I don't have

speaker: CG
words: nothin'! That my house isn't fit to live in, that it isn't right at

speaker: CG
words: at all. The furniture isn't right; nothings right. If I say the truth

speaker: CG
words: of the matter, that I am content with it, on what grounds are they going

speaker: CG
words: to be able to say that I don't have everything? If I felt something

speaker: CG
words: was lacking I couldn't be content. But I say that I have everything,

speaker: CG
words: and I do. That is to say, that I'm content with what I have. And, when

speaker: CG
words: I was a child people were not rife with nerves, like they are now. They

speaker: CG
words: didn't have nerves as they do now. They weren't always busy with some

speaker: CG
words: thing or other. Occasionally there would be someone who kept much bus

speaker: CG
words: ier than anybody else did, and it was noticeable. It was remarkable—

speaker: CG
words: "What was the matter with her, anyway?"

speaker: SS
words: Nerves meaning— you're thinking of someone that was— couldn't sit still

speaker: SS
words: for example?

speaker: CG
words: They could sit still. They could sit quietly down reading the newspaper.

speaker: CG
words: They didn't have to have a Wild West, and the newspapers were clean. And

speaker: CG
words: there wasn't so much reporting of what crime and so on that there was.

speaker: SS
words: What was it you said that a person who was nervous as standing out? What

speaker: SS
words: would a nervous person be lifce ? Someone who couldn't sit still? Who

speaker: SS
words: was

speaker: CG
words: Someone who was wild with nerves was noticeable as one, not as the mass,

speaker: CG
words: but as one, and they were very noticeable. There was a contentment pre

speaker: CG
words: vailing. And, I went through hardtimes, and people took it hard, but

speaker: CG
words: they didn't go wild about it to other people. If they had any wildness

speaker: CG
words: they kept it to themselves. I remember Mother always feeding the men

speaker: CG
words: who came to the door asking for a handout. One time a neighbor woman

speaker: CG
words: told me, "Yes, and someday your mother's going to get killed by one of

speaker: CG
words: those men." Well, I ran home quickly and told her and she said, "No,

speaker: CG
words: no man is as dangerous as a hungry man, so I just feed him." Well, that's

speaker: CG
words: logic. And a man who is hungry is more unfortunate than one who isn't.

speaker: CG
words: Mother just fed them over and over and over.

speaker: SS
words: When you were young and growing up, a man or a woman who was divorced —

speaker: SS
words: was a person divorced regarded as someone not trustworthy?

speaker: CG
words: They were not quite accepted like other people were. They were just a

speaker: CG
words: little on the outer fringe. And divorces were so rare. As much as^ like

speaker: CG
words: sixty years ago a bunch of us were together one day and one of them

speaker: CG
words: spoke about a recent divorce and when the others had threshed it over,

speaker: CG
words: this one woman said, "Well, I always say if I can't get along with the

speaker: CG
words: husband £ have, how do I know that I' U get along with any other one?"

speaker: CG
words: That was the opinion— she had expressed the opinion of the world. That

speaker: CG
words: if you can't do with one partner maybe you wouldn't with any other.

speaker: CG
words: And there was very little divorce. And, I don't remember just when it

speaker: CG
words: was, but I do remember that I began to notice when a man would divorce

speaker: CG
words: his wife, way back when.I was a child and so on, a man wouldn't think

speaker: CG
words: of getting a divorce. He might threaten his wife into her getting a

speaker: CG
words: divorce for his benefit, but he didn't ask for a divorce anymore than

speaker: CG
words: he'd said, "Well, go on open the doors and put my coat on me and do the

speaker: CG
words: things you should." It just wasn't done. But, I noticed in the paper

speaker: CG
words: now and then that a man had divorced his wife.

speaker: SS
words: The age of marriage for young people— was it much different when you

speaker: SS
words: were growing up than now?

speaker: CG
words: I could very well say there was no such thing as double teen marriage.

speaker: CG
words: It's true that a girl might marry at eighteen or nineteen, which was

speaker: CG
words: considered young, and a way back in my grandmother's time in the 1820's

speaker: CG
words: and 30's and 40's, it was considered that a girl should be married be

speaker: CG
words: fore she was twenty, definitely. She must be married in her teens. But

speaker: CG
words: I don't remember any marriage way back in which both were teenage. A

speaker: CG
words: girl might marry a man who could vote, that was a recognized age of some

speaker: CG
words: importance, and it was twenty-one; which should be twenty-five at the

speaker: CG
words: present time, instead of eighteen.

speaker: SS
words: Did young people learn responsibilities at a younger age?

speaker: SS
words: As to what they learned— they learned responsibility about living, where

speaker: SS
words: as now they learn science and all that kind of thing. There was that

speaker: SS
words: sharp difference. The young people might go through high school but

speaker: SS
words: the necessity for taking anything beyond high school— it wasn't consid

speaker: SS
words: ered there was any necessity for it. Of course, some did go to college

speaker: SS
words: but not by hordes. It was a few who went to college. We had a college

speaker: SS
words: in the town in which I lived for some years in my youth, it was known

speaker: SS
words: as Redfield College, which it wasn't, but it was in Redfield, South Dak

speaker: SS
words: ota. First it was in Redfield, Dakota Territory, before the state was

speaker: SS
words: divided. It was a Congregational school maintained by the Congregation

speaker: SS
words: al Church in general, not of the town.but the general conference.

speaker: SS
words: You described to me the story of the—

speaker: CG
words: That college had men and women well up in their twenties and some of them

speaker: CG
words: in their thirties who were learning geography and math and all that kind

speaker: CG
words: of thing, because they hadn't been to high school. And we did have one

speaker: CG
words: little boy there, he was young enough that he was still wearing knee

speaker: CG
words: trousers, that was a mark of whether you were considered adult, was whe

speaker: CG
words: ther you wore knee trousers or could have long ones, and this little

speaker: CG
words: fellow was still wearing the long black stockings and knee trousers.

speaker: CG
words: His mother had died; his father was a pastor of the church in some town

speaker: CG
words: near. And he had this one boy on his hands when his wife died, and he

speaker: CG
words: didn't know how to care for the boy and to go on with his pastorate work.

speaker: CG
words: I think at the present time any boy like that would have been considered

speaker: CG
words: that he could forage for himself. He didn't need any looking after or

speaker: CG
words: anything. But anyway, whether it interfered with his pastorate, or whe

speaker: CG
words: ther he thought it was too lonesome for the boy, or whatever, he sent him

speaker: CG
words: to college, and there he was with this group of men. And I used to think

speaker: CG
words: just how lonesome he must feel in the dorm, at the table, in class, every

speaker: CG
words: where with all grown people. But he was there for the rest of the school

speaker: CG
words: year. I don't know what became of him then.

speaker: SS
words: We talked a little bit about the difference between women on farms and

speaker: SS
words: in town, and I'm curious in a more general way of the difference between

speaker: SS
words: country life and town life in the early days when you were growing up.

speaker: CG
words: Well, for one thing, the farm women in general did not keep up with the

speaker: CG
words: fashions like the town women did. Of course, farming at that time was

speaker: CG
words: not a very lucrative proposition. The crops were poor and the prices

speaker: CG
words: were low, so that in some cases it was impossible— many cases— but in

speaker: CG
words: general, the farm women did not keep up with the fashions like the town

speaker: CG
words: women did. Since it is more difficult to attend school if you live out

speaker: CG
words: of town, than if you live in town where the school is, and the school

speaker: CG
words: that is near you goes only through the eighth grade, —there was a school

speaker: CG
words: on every quarter section— but it went only through the eighth grade,

speaker: CG
words: and in general they did not have as much book education as the town women

speaker: CG
words: did. Although, £Q far as newspapers were concerned, I think they were

speaker: CG
words: receiving more attention from farm women than they were from town women.

speaker: CG
words: The farm woman didn't go to the evening meetings whatever it was, which

speaker: CG
words: would be held in town, but she'd read the paper and she knew what the

speaker: CG
words: news was, and she knew what politics was doing, although I don't know

speaker: CG
words: that women ever talked politics, I never heard them talking it.

speaker: SS
words: But you think she knew what was going on in any case?

speaker: CG
words: I think she knew pretty well whatever was in the newspaper.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think the farm people in the country had a certain amount of dis

speaker: SS
words: trust of the townspeople and their way of living?

speaker: CG
words: Well, the cooking was far different in town than in the country. In the

speaker: CG
words: country if you were mashing the potatoes you put in a big lump of butter

speaker: CG
words: half a cup of cream, in town you put in a quarter of a cup of skimmed

speaker: CG
words: milk. There was that difference in cooking. And, in town now and then

speaker: CG
words: they could buy a steak for a quarter. My father used to go downtown

speaker: CG
words: and buy a quarter's worth of steak and the family was fed steak. And

speaker: CG
words: people on the farms, mostly, did their own butchering so that they had

speaker: CG
words: hams and bacon and steak and roasts and everything. And that made a

speaker: CG
words: big difference in how the cooking went on. And the very fact that the

speaker: CG
words: woman helped with saving the new calves, like taking it into the kitchen

speaker: CG
words: if it was a cold weather calf until it got on it's feet and full of

speaker: CG
words: milk. And she took care of the chickens, and now and then she took the

speaker: CG
words: horses to water. And you know, handling livestock put something into

speaker: CG
words: the system that isn't there otherwise. It makes a little difference.

speaker: CG
words: (Chuckles) Perhaps I should tell you— it's a story of the early, early

speaker: CG
words: days in Moscow, very early days—I heard it when I first came here. It

speaker: CG
words: happened just right down ., across here, on the creek. There was one

speaker: CG
words: house the other side of the creek. The man who lived in it was in the

speaker: CG
words: habit of, after he was through his day's work coming into town and get

speaker: CG
words: ting pretty well hopped up with liquor. But to go home to where he liv

speaker: CG
words: ed across the creek, there was just a log to cross the creek on. So one

speaker: CG
words: time he had gotten pretty well filled up with liquor and he started home

speaker: CG
words: and he got across the log without falling in. It was currently told that

speaker: CG
words: he always fell into the creek. It would be.of course. But this time he

speaker: CG
words: didn't fall in and he went to the door of this one house and knocked and

speaker: CG
words: a woman opened the door and he said, "Lady, would you please tell me

speaker: CG
words: where"— he mentioned his name, "lives?" And she said, "You do, you old

speaker: CG
words: fool. Don't you know your own house?" He said, "No, lady, I don't live

speaker: CG
words: here I didn't fall in the creek]" (Chuckles) That was a favorite story

speaker: CG
words: here in the early days. And, in those early days when I came— that was

speaker: CG
words: early— the old-timers would tell me, "Huh, you've hardly arrived

speaker: CG
words: yet." When I'd been here twenty or thirty years! But Hays Street was

speaker: CG
words: practically thai"edge of the town, and just on the other side of Hays

speaker: CG
words: Street there was a house in a few acres of ground that were cultivated,

speaker: CG
words: right there facing on Hays, and Orchard Avenue was, the whole thing, was

speaker: CG
words: clear out in the country. And the settlement that's just south of the

speaker: CG
words: campus was the King Dairy, and as far south of the creek. There was

speaker: CG
words: a house on the hilL and one or two down closer to the creek.

speaker: SS
words: When you grew up in South Dakota you must have seen much more the tail

speaker: SS
words: end of the real pioneering, because I imagine there was still a lot of

speaker: SS
words: the homesteaders in the country where you grew up.

speaker: CG
words: I have seen a lot of pioneering. I was raised in it in South Dakota—

speaker: CG
words: not South Dakota, Dakota Territory—I was about four when we went onto

speaker: CG
words: Father's homestead there, and that was pioneer, no fooling about that!

speaker: CG
words: We had a large claim house. It was larger than these two rooms a little,

speaker: CG
words: I'd say, but it wasn't as large as the whole house, I would say, and

speaker: CG
words: that was quite a large house. So they had school in our house, and my

speaker: CG
words: Aunt Ellen Connor taught the school. In those times a rule was a rule

speaker: CG
words: and that's it! And it was a rule that a child did not go to school un

speaker: CG
words: til he was six. Well, I was five and my brother was two years younger,

speaker: CG
words: and then we had four older than we were; two girls and two boys. Well,

speaker: CG
words: that four were in school, but Freddie and I were not old enough for

speaker: CG
words: School. And with Mother who went on with her baking or sewing or what

speaker: CG
words: ever, we had one end of the schoolroom and the school was in the other

speaker: CG
words: end. And at one time, a terrible storm, it might have been a blizzard,

speaker: CG
words: I don't remember it, but, anyway, as it struck and before it immediately

speaker: CG
words: got thick, everybody was started home. Aunt Ellen, who was teaching,

speaker: CG
words: and her youngest brother was in school, and her oldest brothers' older

speaker: CG
words: children were in school. That was the school. And they were all started

speaker: CG
words: home, all got home. And I saw pioneer life there. And I saw pioneer

speaker: CG
words: life in North Dakota in the end of the 1800's. And there the people

speaker: CG
words: were very interesting. There was the Slav-Russian and the German-Rus

speaker: CG
words: sian and the Scandinavians and here and there there were some Americans.

speaker: CG
words: But they were not too common. And one of the Slav-Russians had built

speaker: CG
words: his house, I think as they did in Russia, of sod, and as they did in

speaker: CG
words: all of the new countries; built of sod, because there wouldn't be any

speaker: CG
words: thing else. And a sod house properly put up is a real house. The bliz

speaker: CG
words: zards don't go through it, and the heat of the sun in summer don't go

speaker: CG
words: through it. But they had built their house of sod and they had built

speaker: CG
words: the windows right in with the sod. They didn't open at all. And the

speaker: CG
words: only way into a house was through the door that opened in through the

speaker: CG
words: barn. You went through the barn, down the alley between the two rows

speaker: CG
words: of stock, and then you got into the house where the windows could not

speaker: CG
words: be opened, and that was the only door.^ My brother was in a harvest crew

speaker: CG
words: that traded work at this place. At that time, for harvest or anything

speaker: CG
words: that required more men, they traded work. They didn't hire, and he was

speaker: CG
words: in the crew that was trading work. And he said it was just almost more

speaker: CG
words: than he could live through to sit at the table while they had dinner!

speaker: CG
words: But that's the way they lived. One of the girls was in my school.

speaker: CG
words: Her hair was black, but actually her hair was gray with the lice that

speaker: CG
words: were in it! And she just endured it. The Slav-Russian is of a very

speaker: CG
words: low walked on order. They have never had any opportunity at all, and

speaker: CG
words: they are a very low people. But of the German-Russians— I had in one

speaker: CG
words: school a little girl named Amanda Markmakowski. Her father had died,

speaker: CG
words: her mother was Russian, so she spoke Russian. And her stepfather was

speaker: CG
words: German, so she spoke German, and she spoke both of the languages. She

speaker: CG
words: was eight years old. She spoke both of the languages fluently, and

speaker: CG
words: spoke English without any accent of either of her other languages. So

speaker: CG
words: there she was at eight years competent with three languages. She had

speaker: CG
words: big gray eyes, and when she stood to recite, she looked straight into

speaker: CG
words: my eyes and never turned her gaze once, and whatever I said she could

speaker: CG
words: repeat verbatim any time afterwards. She took it all. I never had

speaker: CG
words: such a remarkable child as she was.

speaker: SS
words: I didn't know that you taught school.

speaker: CG
words: Oh, I taught for seven years before I married. I taught in South Dakota

speaker: CG
words: and North Dakota and Montana. And in Montana, it was new there when I

speaker: CG
words: there, not the really new, but they told me a good many of the

speaker: CG
words: early day tales, and they would tell me about Brother Van. Brother Van

speaker: CG
words: was the Reverend Willian Van Orsdell. He was the first Methodist mis

speaker: CG
words: sionary ever to go to Montana Territory. Well, it was a state at the

speaker: CG
words: time I lived there, but it had been a territory when he there.

speaker: CG
words: And he got with the people to the extent that he had no name but Brother

speaker: CG
words: Van, to such an extent that in his final illness he said, "Now on my

speaker: CG
words: stone put nothing but two dates and the words Brother Van." So his stone

speaker: CG
words: is marked with two dates and Brother Van. And on Rock Creek, that was

speaker: CG
words: settled a little more than Trout Creek where I lived, and they were a

speaker: CG
words: slightly different set of people. And he went there preaching at cer

speaker: CG
words: tain intervals— stated intervals— and everybody would turn out for

speaker: CG
words: the preaching. And they wanted him to come and have Thanksgiving din

speaker: CG
words: ner. In that neighborhood the people all got together every Thanksgiv

speaker: CG
words: ing Day and had a general dinner. And they wanted him to join them.

speaker: CG
words: And he said, "Well, I'll tell you what I'll do." He said, "If you'll

speaker: CG
words: build a church here, like you ought to, I'll have Thanksgiving dinner

speaker: CG
words: with you every year as long as I live." Which he did as long as he was

speaker: CG
words: able to travel that far. He got a horse, he went horseback just as other

speaker: CG
words: people did.

speaker: SS
words: Did he have to ride^circuit? Did he have to ride a circuit? Did he

speaker: SS
words: have a wide area to cover?

speaker: CG
words: He rode a circuit, and he rode a horse. And he got in with the Indians.

speaker: CG
words: There wasn't anybody he couldn't make friends with. And the Indians

speaker: CG
words: took him on a buffalo hunt, and they waited very politely for him to

speaker: CG
words: shoot the first buffalo, which he did. And then they, with their arrows

speaker: CG
words: got their buffalo. But he was riding on a part of his circuit one

speaker: CG
words: night, it was dark and rain was pouring, and he thought, "Well, nobody

speaker: CG
words: is going to go to that schoolhouse a night like this. If I see a light

speaker: CG
words: anywhere I'm going to stop and ask to spend the night." Well, he saw

speaker: CG
words: a light and went to the door and knocked and the girl with a shotgun in

speaker: CG
words: her hands came to the door. He introduced himself and asked if he could

speaker: CG
words: spend the night. She said, "No, you can't." She said, "We have only

speaker: CG
words: one spare bed and we're keeping that for J)pother Van, and we can't keep

speaker: CG
words: you. And my mother and father are at the schoolhouse now waiting for

speaker: CG
words: Brother Van." So he got back in the saddle and went on in a hurry.

speaker: CG
words: And they took him home with them at the close of the meeting and this

speaker: CG
words: girl he became engaged to. And just days before they were to be mar

speaker: CG
words: ried, without any explanation that anybody could think of she died. And

speaker: CG
words: he never paid the slightest attention to any woman specially, you know,

speaker: CG
words: after that. Just lived by himself and went on with his work. And at

speaker: CG
words: one time someone hunted up Brother Van in a hurry and they told him

speaker: CG
words: that the Indians had taken a certain man captive and that they were camp

speaker: CG
words: ed about two miles from Gregory Flat, and would he be able to do anything

speaker: CG
words: about getting the man? Well, he got into his saddle and went out there

speaker: CG
words: in a hurry and when he got to Gregory Flat he dropped the reins so his

speaker: CG
words: horse would stay there and started this two miles back to where the

speaker: CG
words: Indians would be camped.

speaker: CG
words: And when he got back the dogs started barking and he knew he'd been heard, that the

speaker: CG
words: dogs knew he was coming and there

speaker: CG
words: for knew that something was doing and he started singing. He loved to

speaker: CG
words: sing. He had a sort of a tenor voice, not a clear high tenor,

speaker: CG
words: but a very good tenor voice, and he did love to sing. And he started

speaker: CG
words: singing when the dogs started barking. Well, when he got to the camp

speaker: CG
words: the Indians were sitting in a circle around the^campfire, and he just

speaker: CG
words: simply went and sat down among them and sat there until the chief began

speaker: CG
words: after a while to talk to him. The Chief passed the peace pipe and he

speaker: CG
words: took his pull on the peace pipe and passed it on. And after a while

speaker: CG
words: the Chief spoke to him, and he said what he had come to to see

speaker: CG
words: this man, and he let the Indian lead the talking and he'd talk a little

speaker: CG
words: now and then and he seemed to know just the thing to say. So after a

speaker: CG
words: while the Chief told someone to go and cut the man's bindings loose.

speaker: CG
words: So the man got on his feet and the Chief told  he could go now

speaker: CG
words: and take the man with him. So they left. That was Brother Van. And

speaker: CG
words: there the story, I didn't hear it direct, but I've heard it— but

speaker: CG
words: this I heard so direct that there was no question on the story at all.

speaker: CG
words: But it was said that Brother Van always wore his pulpit clothing so

speaker: CG
words: that they would recognize him as a divine. And he was wearing his pul

speaker: CG
words: pit clothing and a posse came along hunting for a horse thief and one

speaker: CG
words: of the posse said, "That's the man." So they seized Brother Van. And

speaker: CG
words: he said, no, he wasn't the thief "We'll hang you anyway and then we'll

speaker: CG
words: ask the questions when we get you hung." Well, he said he wasn't the

speaker: CG
words: thief, he was Brother Van. And one of the men said, "Well, I know how

speaker: CG
words: we can tell whether he's Brother Van or not. I've heard him sing,"—

speaker: CG
words: I can't think of what the story was, the hymn—"but I've heard him sing

speaker: CG
words: that. Let him sing it now and I'll know whether that's Brother Van or

speaker: CG
words: not." So Brother Van started on the hymn and after a little this man

speaker: CG
words: said, "That is Brother Van. There is no question about it!" And this

speaker: CG
words: time he wasn't wearing his pulpit suit for some reason. But after that

speaker: CG
words: he never was out without his pulpit suit on.

speaker: SS
words: What part of Montana was this? Where in Montana?

speaker: CG
words: It was the other side of Great Falls, between Great Falls and Lewistown

speaker: CG
words: and and Harlow

speaker: SS
words: What was it like being a teacher during those years that you taught in

speaker: SS
words: the little schoolhouses? I imagine that conditions must have been

speaker: SS
words: fairly rugged then?

speaker: CG
words: Well, they were rugged but we were rugged, tool And you can believe

speaker: CG
words: it or not, my very first school I had twenty-five dollars a month, my

speaker: CG
words: own, twenty-five dollars month! And I had about a dozen pupils, I

speaker: CG
words: think, there might have been a bit more than that, but not much, if

speaker: CG
words: there was any . And, at that time, they handed you a contract

speaker: CG
words: and a broom and a fire shovel and you were all ready to teach! So,

speaker: CG
words: they told me I could board with Mrs. Mead. So I went over to Mrs.

speaker: CG
words: Mead's and they told me it was a little over a mile— about a mile—

speaker: CG
words: well, to me a mile was nothing, I could walk it in fifteen minutes

speaker: CG
words: without hurrying. And, I went over and she said, yes, she would keep

speaker: CG
words: me for ten dollars a month. Well, that wasn't my first school, that

speaker: CG
words: was my second, but, I said, "I paid only eight dollars at my other

speaker: CG
words: school." She said, "Yes, but I have to have ten dollars and I'll do

speaker: CG
words: your washing, too." Well, that was something. So, for ten dollars I

speaker: CG
words: had my board and lodging and washing! And then I had fifteen left.

speaker: CG
words: And we had readin , writin, and 'rithmetic, that's what we had. And

speaker: CG
words: if they were old enough, they had a geography book. And you were just

speaker: CG
words: somebody when you got to the place where you could have a geography

speaker: CG
words: book! But, you had a reader and you had a speller— and could they

speaker: CG
words: spell! They learned the letters that went into each word, and they

speaker: CG
words: knew how to say them. And, I'd tap my bell and the next class would

speaker: CG
words: come up and stand in front of me—I was sitting on my rostrum, about a

speaker: CG
words: foot or a little higher, maybe eighteen inches high— I was sitting at

speaker: CG
words: my desk up there and they stood before me and they recited for fifteen

speaker: CG
words: minutes and I tapped my bell and they went and sat down and went on

speaker: CG
words: with their work. And they had good times together. There was one

speaker: CG
words: school I taught in which these little Slav-Russian boys came in and

speaker: CG
words: they were crying, and they said, "The girls won't let me play." And,

speaker: CG
words: I said, I knew why— I said, "Why don't they let you play?" They said

speaker: CG
words: our hands are too dirty. I said, "Well, when you go home, you wash your

speaker: CG
words: hands with soap until they're just real clean all over, and the girls'

speaker: CG
words: let you play tomorrow." Well, they did, they had orders! But, oh,

speaker: CG
words: their hands! You can't imagine^a human being could be as dirty as those

speaker: CG
words: boys were. And, they did, they washed their hands, but they didn't go

speaker: CG
words: any farther. (Chuckles) But I felt sorry for the poor little youngsters.

speaker: SS
words: Did you find that there were wide differences in ability among the child

speaker: SS
words: ren you taught? Did you find among the children that you taught that

speaker: SS
words: there were wide differences? In ability?

speaker: CG
words: Yes, there was. Yes, this second school that I spoke of— there was a

speaker: CG
words: boy who— well, I wouldn't say he wasn't bright, he was just an ordinary

speaker: CG
words: youngster, but he couldn't take books as fast as the rest of them did,

speaker: CG
words: and I was too inexperienced to recognize the problem for what it was,

speaker: CG
words: and  could have been done. But, he was a nice little boy and about

speaker: CG
words: living, you didn't see any difference in him and any of the rest of them.

speaker: CG
words: At another school I had a girl that came to school just a little while,

speaker: CG
words: she was the oldest of the family and she was ten or eleven at this time.

speaker: CG
words: And because she had never been in school enough to learn to use a book,

speaker: CG
words: it was very difficult for her, and she came just a little while. They

speaker: CG
words: were Russian, but not Slav* And, I did have some that were

speaker: CG
words: really bright. And I had one boy that stammered quite badly. And I

speaker: CG
words: wrote a note to his mother and asked her if there was something I could

speaker: CG
words: do to help him get over it. She sent back a note saying, "Let him alone,

speaker: CG
words: don't say anything about it. Just let him alone." Well, I'm not sure

speaker: CG
words: that was the thing to do, but I did. But he did stammer badly, and that

speaker: CG
words: held him backward, because he was so selfconscious. The brother older

speaker: CG
words: was one of those people that just takes with everybody; he was a wonder

speaker: CG
words: ful boy. And then they had— then they had two-- well, they really were

speaker: CG
words: adult sisters—they were, I think fifteen and seventeen. They were the

speaker: CG
words: two first of the children. And they had six children in school and they

speaker: CG
words: had two more at home at the same time, and I heard afterward that they

speaker: CG
words: had one or two more than that. Their farming wasn't panning out at all,

speaker: CG
words: and they were living on practically nothing. But the mother was a very

speaker: CG
words: superior person, very superior. And she took in sewing^ And did those

speaker: CG
words: youngsters know how to work! They'd better learn] I know I was there

speaker: CG
words: one time when she asked the oldest girl to do something or other and

speaker: CG
words: the girl was dipping something into something and she turned to one of

speaker: CG
words: the others and she says, "Hand me that—" And the mother said, "Ollie,

speaker: CG
words: when you start something, before you start it you say to yourself, "Now

speaker: CG
words: what am I going to need while I do that." And you get it." That's the

speaker: CG
words: way she brought her youngsters up. And they were nice children. And

speaker: CG
words: the father was such a good soul. Slow, but so good.

speaker: SS
words: Was the lot of this family unusual, or was it common that the families

speaker: SS
words: there were having a rough time makinga go of it?

speaker: CG
words: Well, six at that time was not considered a large family. If you went

speaker: CG
words: beyond six, it was getting a little sizeable, but six was not a large

speaker: CG
words: family.

speaker: SS
words: I mean, their lot, as far as having a hard time getting along, like they

speaker: SS
words: were having? Was that common for families in the area? Was everybody

speaker: SS
words: struggling? Most people struggling?

speaker: CG
words: Nearly all of them. Nearly all of them were having a struggle. For

speaker: CG
words: one thing, it just wasn't a crop climate. There was a time there in the

speaker: CG
words: earliest days that the annual rainfall was fourteen inches. Well, that's

speaker: CG
words: supposed to be below anything that will raise a crop. And they hadn't

speaker: CG
words: gotten onto how to farm as much as they did later. And then later I

speaker: CG
words: heard one year, in a letter, about that the crops were being rained out.

speaker: CG
words: I said, "Well, I'll believe that when I see it!" Well, in that same

speaker: CG
words: summer or the next, I was on a train from Huron to Redfield and in build

speaker: CG
words: ing the railroads they always made a ditch alongside, and it was gumbo

speaker: CG
words: land; and gumbo holds the rain almost like plastic would, and right be

speaker: CG
words: side the track in this ditch, that was about that wide, I think, corn

speaker: CG
words: was planted and water was standing inches deep on the corn and the corn

speaker: CG
words: was yellow and puny. I've seen crops rained out.

speaker: SS
words: What area was this that you're describings what part of Dakota?

speaker: CG
words: It was— well, I began in Falk County. All my teaching was in Falk

speaker: CG
words: County,Amy certificate was from Falk County. And it's way east of the

speaker: CG
words: Missouri River, Aberdeen on the north and Huron on the south,— I guess

speaker: CG
words: Huron was about as much south as it was east—but there was Brookings

speaker: CG
words: and Redfield was the town for that part.

speaker: SS
words: Well, were most of the people on the farms still optimistic of being

speaker: SS
words: able to be successful, or was that period of boom already past?

speaker: CG
words: In the very early days, there was a stream of prairie schooners, that is

speaker: CG
words: covered wagons, going West and a stream of them going East. And one of

speaker: CG
words: them that was going East had a sign on it that said, "Going back to wife's

speaker: CG
words: folks!" (Chuckles) So that was for some years like that.

speaker: CG
words: And then, after a while, I think people began to believe that they were

speaker: CG
words: going to make a success of it.

speaker: SS
words: I think one more thing about this teaching—. What made you decide to

speaker: SS
words: become a teacher?

speaker: CG
words: Because my older sister compelled me to (Chuckles) She knew what was

speaker: CG
words: proper for me! She had the idea— I wanted to be a nurse, I wanted to

speaker: CG
words: take training. I might even see a man in his bed if I nursed! She

speaker: CG
words: wasn't going to have me in any occupation like that! So I had to teach.

speaker: CG
words: And, I guess I made a success of it. I had a letter from the chairman

speaker: CG
words: of the board of directors of one school that I might teach his school

speaker: CG
words: anytime I wanted to. And nobody complained that I know.

speaker: SS
words: Was it hard for you to teach because it was not what you wanted to do?

speaker: SS
words: Or did you just go ahead and do it anyway?

speaker: CG
words: No, I liked it. I liked it. I enjoyed it. I've done quite a lot of

speaker: CG
words: kinds of work. Enough that at eighty-two I matriculated over here at

speaker: CG
words: the University (Idaho) In this little space at the bottom that says

speaker: CG
words: "What occupations have you followed?" I filled all of that space and

speaker: CG
words: went all around all the margins with different jobs I'd worked at!

speaker: CG
words: But I can't say that there were any of them that I objected to, really.

speaker: CG
words: They were all right; it was work and they had remuneration, to some ex

speaker: CG
words: tent, at least. I don't think I hated anything I did, no matter what

speaker: CG
words: it was. And in my teaching, well, there were interesting things that

speaker: CG
words: came up, pleasant things and sometimes there would be something funny.

speaker: CG
words: I really enjoyed it. In one quizz I had asked the question, Name some

speaker: CG
words: natural curiosity of South Dakota. One boy wrote cabbages and apples!

speaker: CG
words: He was right. Cabbages and apples were very scarce. (Chuckles) But

speaker: CG
words: it was an odd reply.

speaker: SS
words: Talking about the circuit riding. I was wondering, did you ever attend

speaker: SS
words: a revival? A religious revival?

speaker: CG
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: What were they like then?

speaker: CG
words: Well, I attended one that impressed me more than any other one. It was

speaker: CG
words: in a church, we had a small church at Garneill, Montana, and the people

speaker: CG
words: of the little settlement were there and there were some people who had

speaker: CG
words: come some miles to attend the meeting. This was not I think Rever

speaker: CG
words: end Van it was not Brother Van. The building was filled to a mea

speaker: CG
words: sure that it was physically filled— not only myself—but everybody felt

speaker: CG
words: the Holy Spirit filling that building. Everybody was held spellbound,

speaker: CG
words: and one man rose to testify; he was just an uneducated sheepherder, and

speaker: CG
words: he rose and faced us, he had his cap in his hands, and he rolled his cap

speaker: CG
words: over and over and over and the preacher said, "Well, the brother has no

speaker: CG
words: thing-he can say to us." The man was totally unable to say one word.

speaker: CG
words: But he'd made his testimony. My brother told me of a meeting in a town

speaker: CG
words: in Montana— I wish that I could remember the name of that town, but I

speaker: CG
words: can't— it's toward Great Falls, not so far out of Great Falls, they

speaker: CG
words: were having a revival there, and this brother of mine and two uncles

speaker: CG
words: went in from where they were camped in their prairie schooner, and attend

speaker: CG
words: ed the meeting. And the brother said that when the altar call was made

speaker: CG
words: the people just sat. There was no movement whatever, they just sat.

speaker: CG
words: And the pastor tried talking; he wasn't getting anywhere. And these two

speaker: CG
words: uncles of mine, one of them was very dignified and quiet and backward,

speaker: CG
words: and the other one was of the cannonball type. In fact, he was elected

speaker: CG
words: to the legislature at the time the workingmen were trying to put over the

speaker: CG
words: eight hour law, and to the time he died he had one of the pens with which

speaker: CG
words: the Governor signed the bill, and they put him in to work for the bill.

speaker: CG
words: And it was largely his work that got it in. Well, he was just that can

speaker: CG
words: nonball type. He could just be sitting and he'd just rise up and every

speaker: CG
words: body would notice him. And he'd start to speak and they'd just sit there

speaker: CG
words: with their eyes on him listening. So when the preacher was having no suc

speaker: CG
words: cess with his altar call, Uncle Charlie just simply got up and started

speaker: CG
words: talking and my brother said that in just minutes— two or three minutes—

speaker: CG
words: they altar was full of  people! And he, himself,— I don't know

speaker: CG
words: that he ever claimed to be Christian, or whether he didn't. I don't

speaker: CG
words: know. But, anyway, just the fact that he was talking— He's the only

speaker: CG
words: one anywhere in the whole connection that I ever knew being like that.

speaker: CG
words: I do have a nephew who makes friends with everybody. He told me one

speaker: CG
words: time, it's a fact, he said he could stand on a street corner in any city

speaker: CG
words: in the United States and inside of an hour somebody would pass him, stand

speaker: CG
words: ing on the street corner, that he knew from reading about him, or had met

speaker: CG
words: or personally f(new, he said he had tried it, and there never was a time

speaker: CG
words: but in an hour that person would pass. And the memory he had. He could

speaker: CG
words: go back to the time he was four or five years old and tell you exactly

speaker: CG
words: what had happened at what place, when. And one time someone— I had a

speaker: CG
words: bunch of college men eating here one time and my nephew was here a whileand

speaker: CG
words: one time one of them mentioned a baseball game some years back, something

speaker: CG
words: special about it that they'd been talking and he'd mentioned

speaker: CG
words: this, and this nephew of mine spoke up and said, "Yes, and the score was

speaker: CG
words: " He named the two teams and the score of each of them several years

speaker: CG
words: back. I never saw anything like it.

speaker: SS
words: Before you get off the subject of revivals, I wanted to know more about

speaker: SS
words: what that revival was like, the one you mentioned that was so moving.

speaker: SS
words: Because we have little record in this area of what they revivals were

speaker: SS
words: like. What was involved in them.

speaker: CG
words: These people were stricken with it, with the Spirit, but there was no re

speaker: CG
words: sponse to the altar call.

speaker: SS
words: No, I don't mean the one with your brother, I mean the one before that.

speaker: SS
words: You described being in the hall with the feeling of the Holy Spirit.

speaker: CG
words: That was in a church. Well, that's the one I mean. That's the one I

speaker: CG
words: mean when I say that the people were stricken, there was no response,

speaker: CG
words: which I couldn't understand. But that's the way it was.

speaker: SS
words: How did they usually go? Were they long, the revivals? Did they last

speaker: SS
words: for days, or just for a short period of time?

speaker: CG
words: They might last for, oh, two or three or four evenings, or they might

speaker: CG
words: be just one evening. Frequently were only one evening, more frequently.

speaker: CG
words: And then there were the camp meetings, and they lasted a week. And at

speaker: CG
words: the camp meetings— well, they just had a series of service right down

speaker: CG
words: through the day. Made use of all the time they were there. But it was

speaker: CG
words: a session of classes with learning and^the great speakers of the nation

speaker: CG
words: speaking on whatever that speaker's topic was. They were an educational

speaker: CG
words: affair, and they were quite largely attended. That was a paid attend

speaker: CG
words: ance. You paid for attending.

speaker: SS
words: To think of what Christianity was like in those days; for instance, was

speaker: SS
words: the Sabbath observed very strictly?

speaker: CG
words: With many people it was. My grandmother observed it so strictly that one

speaker: CG
words: day when my sister said to her, "Grandmother, are you going to wash to

speaker: CG
words: morrow?" And Grandmother said, "I try to observe the Sabbath." She

speaker: CG
words: wouldn't say yes or no! And there was— when I lived near Garneill,—

speaker: CG
words: there was a Phillipps family there that the mother and the sons went on

speaker: CG
words: running the farm after the father died. And one day a neighbor man rode

speaker: CG
words: over and Mrs. Phillipps was a great worker, and here was Mrs. Phillipps

speaker: CG
words: all dressed up in her Sunday best, and he wondered how that was, but she

speaker: CG
words: asked the man in and took him into the parlor and here'-sat the P.hilipps

speaker: CG
words: family in their Sunday best sitting around the room, and he talked a

speaker: CG
words: little while and Mrs. Phillipps was just as usual and everybody else

speaker: CG
words: was, only they were just sitting dressed up. And finally, he said,"Mrs.

speaker: CG
words: Phillipps is there something wrong, has something happened to you? Is

speaker: CG
words: anything the matter?" She said, "Well, why do you ask?" "Well," he

speaker: CG
words: said, "I was surprised when you folks work the fields continuously that

speaker: CG
words: you were all sitting here dressed up." She said, "I alwa*- observe the

speaker: CG
words: Sabbath that way." He said, "Yes, but this is Monday!" (Laughter) And

speaker: CG
words: she said, "Well, anyway we observed the Sabbath. Go get your work clothes

speaker: CG
words: on and go to work!" (More laughter) She was quite a woman.

speaker: SS
words: In the use of scripture then— I get the idea that scripture and the

speaker: SS
words: quotation, the citing of scripture, was much more important then than in

speaker: SS
words: Christianity today. Is that true?

speaker: CG
words: That is true. We had the King James Version of the Scripture and we

speaker: CG
words: didn't need anything else to go with it. We used that, and it was very

speaker: CG
words: common and most of the homes had a great, big, family bible. A book

speaker: CG
words: about that thick, probably. Something like that size. And it had the

speaker: CG
words: King James version of the Bible and then it had blank leaves for family

speaker: CG
words: records and all that. And that was very common. We never knew what

speaker: CG
words: became of ours. I had a sister who married a widower, and when my sis

speaker: CG
words: ter died his children went in and took what they wanted and burned the

speaker: CG
words: rest. And we don't know whether they took the bible or burned it. And

speaker: CG
words: my sister had Grandmother's sidesaddle, be worth five thousand dollars

speaker: CG
words: right now, whether they took or burned it, we don't know. But, any

speaker: CG
words: way that's the way things go. You can just say with belief in it that

speaker: CG
words: nothing is safe; anything is likely to happen.

speaker: SS
words: The preacher that you were telling me about— the preacher who you were

speaker: SS
words: just telling me about before that had all the adventures and was so well

speaker: SS
words: known—

speaker: CG
words: Van Orsdell.

speaker: SS
words: Did you see him?

speaker: CG
words: Oh, yes, I knew him personally. At one time he and I were both on the

speaker: CG
words: train from Moore to Lewistown—it isn't Lewiston, it's Lewistown— we

speaker: CG
words: were both on the train. He was sitting in the back of the coach with

speaker: CG
words: a bunch of men and they were having a good time laughing and joking and

speaker: CG
words: as we all rose to go out when the train got to Lewistown, I waited f@r

speaker: CG
words: the jam to get out of the door, I was in no hurry, like jamming with

speaker: CG
words: the rest of them, so I simply stood until a little of the crush would be

speaker: CG
words: by, and he came along and he was a great, big, tall man, and he stooped

speaker: CG
words: down over me and he said, "Oh, Sister, I am so frightened, can you tell

speaker: CG
words: me which bus to take?" (Chuckles) Well, there was one cab, and he had

speaker: CG
words: said, "can you tell me which cab to take?" There was one horsedrawn cab

speaker: CG
words: in town! (Chuckles) And he'd known the place when it was just a vil

speaker: CG
words: lage. But that was Brother Van. He always had to have his fun.

speaker: SS
words: What was he like to listen to as a preacher?

speaker: CG
words: He didn't have sermons. He'd get up to preach a sermon and— well, he'd

speaker: CG
words: quote some scripture and maybe he'd make a few remarks and he'd say some

speaker: CG
words: thing like, "I know you boys are having a hard time, but be as good as

speaker: CG
words: you can, won't you?" And then he'd start singing. And everybody would

speaker: CG
words: join in and they'd sing a hymn, and then he'd talk a little longer, and

speaker: CG
words: he'd hope their crops were coming well— he didn't preach a sermon at

speaker: CG
words: all. But he did put some very pointed remarks in here and there that fit

speaker: CG
words: ted, and he knew how to fit them. But you couldn't say that he ever

speaker: CG
words: preached a sermon. But he did love to sing. His favorite song was,

speaker: CG
words: "Beyond the smiling and the weeping, I shall be soon." Well, he wasn't

speaker: CG
words: very soon, his girlfriend wasl but he was getting I think, pretty well

speaker: CG
words: into the seventies perhaps, I'm not sure, just how old he was, but he

speaker: CG
words: wasn't a young man by any means.

speaker: SS
words: These stories that you were telling about him, I take it that these were

speaker: SS
words: stories that were fairly well known by the congregation?

speaker: CG
words: Yes. Yes, just common talk.

speaker: SS
words: The time that he came to the house at night and then went on to the meet

speaker: SS
words: ing from there, did you ever hear whether there were many people at that

speaker: SS
words: meeting?

speaker: CG
words: No, I never did.

speaker: SS
words: Her parents were there anyway.

speaker: CG
words: Her parents were there. No, I never heard whether there were other peo

speaker: CG
words: ple or nothing about it, because they'd told the story; that was it.

speaker: SS
words: Was there much talk about going to Heaven or being damned, when you were

speaker: SS
words: young? In the services in church? Being saved?

speaker: CG
words: Well, distinctly, there was a Heaven and a Hell, and some prechexs har

speaker: CG
words: ped on the two and other preachers didn't mention them. In Redfield, at

speaker: CG
words: one time, we had an extremely remarkable man. For his sermon that was

speaker: CG
words: preached the Sunday that— of the teachers' meeting when the certificates*-

speaker: CG
words: the examinations were made and the certificates were awarded, th:? group

speaker: CG
words: practically all went to his church Sunday morning, and knowing that they

speaker: CG
words: would be there, he had a collection of small stones that he had picked

speaker: CG
words: up here and there, there were plenty. And he compared life to those

speaker: CG
words: stones until— I wasn't at church that Sunday, my sister was— and she

speaker: CG
words: said as they went out the teachers were all saying wasn't that the most

speaker: CG
words: remarkable sermon you ever heard? Did you ever hear a better sermon?

speaker: CG
words: He had just captivated his audience with it. And he was very much given

speaker: CG
words: to small words that are in common use. He didn't have to use five or

speaker: CG
words: six syllables to a word to express himself. He just talked plain every

speaker: CG
words: day language in a plain everyday way. But he did preach remarkable ser

speaker: CG
words: mons and they were real sermons. But, I can't remember him ever talking

speaker: CG
words: about Heaven and Hell. He brought it right to the people today. He had

speaker: CG
words: come out on recommendation of a doctor that he bring his wife to a dif

speaker: CG
words: ferent climate. But she died in spite of all that could be done for her.

speaker: CG
words: He made himself very unpopular by, later on, I don't know, a couple of

speaker: CG
words: years or so, he married his wife's nitce, who had taken care of his wife

speaker: CG
words: in her last time. Well, his wife's miace was no relation to him, and

speaker: CG
words: there were some of the people who couldn there was anything

speaker: CG
words: wrong in him marrying her, but some of them were very bitter about it.

speaker: CG
words: But, he was a remarkable man.

speaker: SS
words: When you say his sermons were remarkable: does that mean that when this

speaker: SS
words: idea, that he would get it down to the people and make it mean something

speaker: SS
words: in their lives?

speaker: CG
words: He could reach them. Yes, whatever he said, reached the people. They

speaker: CG
words: understood it. And I think they took it to heart. I know he was very

speaker: CG
words: popular up to the time he made his second marriage.

speaker: SS
words: I'm trying to understand more about the difference between the country

speaker: SS
words: and town in the early days. And, I've had the idea from things people

speaker: SS
words: have said, towns often were not as friendly, to country people when they

speaker: SS
words: moved into town. The towns were in some ways more closed than neigh

speaker: SS
words: borhoods that were friendly and open. Is that true, do you think? Or

speaker: SS
words: does that just depend too much?

speaker: CG
words: I think perhaps that wasn't true to any great extent. It might have

speaker: CG
words: been localized here and there to a marked degree, but in general I would

speaker: CG
words: n't think there was such a great difference. And, there wasn't so much

speaker: CG
words: of country people moving into town as there is now. They probably just

speaker: CG
words: stayed on the farm until they died.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that town people were more caught up in the idea of getting

speaker: SS
words: ahead and getting a better status in life than country people?

speaker: CG
words: No. No, I don't. It's true that they might make more display but I

speaker: CG
words: think the country people were just as ambitious and just as much inter

speaker: CG
words: ested in getting on as any town people could be. But, a person might

speaker: CG
words: have an idea from the fact that back in those early days the farming

speaker: CG
words: was so far from being remunerative as it has been at times since, that

speaker: CG
words: the people had no opportunity to get ahead as perhaps someone in the

speaker: CG
words: town did. And so they might have thought the town people were more am

speaker: CG
words: bitious with the people in the country working about eighteen hours a

speaker: CG
words: day and hoping and doing everything they could to get ahead. Near Redfield

speaker: CG
words: there was one farmer— and why others didn't take a pattern from

speaker: CG
words: him, I don't know—he planted wheat, oats, barley, speltz, corn, and he

speaker: CG
words: planted all the crops, about so much of it. Well, some of it would fail

speaker: CG
words: and some of it didn't. Every year, there was never a year that he didn't

speaker: CG
words: have some crops that gave him a return. And he had horses and mules and

speaker: CG
words: cattle and sheep and hogs and chickens and turkeys and ducks and geese.

speaker: CG
words: People would sometimes laugh-.at him about having such a mixture, he'd

speaker: CG
words: say, "Well, I get something, if I don't get it from one I get it from

speaker: CG
words: another." And yet, nobody took a pattern from him and he wasn't having

speaker: CG
words: a hard time. He was well-to-do. His son was going to college. Well,

speaker: CG
words: that meant that he could pay the college expenses and hire someone to

speaker: CG
words: take the son's place on the farm. It didn't worry him any. He really

speaker: CG
words: made a success of what he did.

speaker: SS
words: This idea of becoming successful and getting ahead in the early days—

speaker: SS
words: if farming wasn't paying well, then it seems like almost everybody that

speaker: SS
words: farmed wouldn't be doing too well until things changed quite a bit. And

speaker: SS
words: seems like— I wonder why many more people didn't become discouraged, or

speaker: SS
words: if they somehow believed in themselves that they were going to get ahead

speaker: SS
words: some day if they stuck with it long enough.

speaker: CG
words: People at that time were more stalwart than they are now, and they did

speaker: CG
words: n't become discouraged as much as people do now. They didn't become any

speaker: CG
words: thing extrovert as much as people do now. They had a trail that they

speaker: CG
words: followed, and if there was no trail there, they made one, and you just

speaker: CG
words: couldn't move them like you can people now. You didn't hear of all this

speaker: CG
words: moving around and so on. And, at that time, practically all of the old

speaker: CG
words: people, when they needed care were cared for by their own. It is true

speaker: CG
words: right now that in order to be employed, a great many men must move fre

speaker: CG
words: quently. And it is true, that when he gets into this next town, a one

speaker: CG
words: room apartment or at best, a one bedroom apartment is what he can pay

speaker: CG
words: for. Well, you can't take your parents into a place like that. People

speaker: CG
words: could before. I know a place in which a man and his wife, when they

speaker: CG
words: were old, were living with their son, and I'm sure they had only a one

speaker: CG
words: room shanty, and they lived there until the man died there. And he died

speaker: CG
words: on the third of July. Well, his body had to lie right there until burial

speaker: CG
words: so, they buried him the morning of the fourth of July. And the people

speaker: CG
words: all came to the funeral before they went to celebrate. But, there they

speaker: CG
words: lived, that old couple and the son in that one room shanty and there he

speaker: CG
words: died, and there he lay until they could bury him. Those things were not

speaker: CG
words: so uncommon. The trouble with the people now, in my opinion, began with

speaker: CG
words: the dole. The oh, what ?

speaker: SS
words: The WPA?

speaker: CG
words: Yes. The WPA and that kind of thing. That was handed out to the people

speaker: CG
words: to break their backbones and it did. And seeing that that broke their

speaker: CG
words: backbones, they've done more until they've broken us all up. But we had

speaker: CG
words: no such thing then. Our near ancestors, we didn't go way back to find

speaker: CG
words: it, the next generation back of us had probably been absolutely pioneers

speaker: CG
words: to a place where there was nothing, to where no human being had ever

speaker: CG
words: been. Well, you have to have backbone if you go to that place and come

speaker: CG
words: out. And a good many lived there and either got out and pioneered else

speaker: CG
words: where, or lost the pioneer spirit and stayed on what they had there.

speaker: CG
words: But they had a backbone that the people now don't know anything about at

speaker: CG
words: all. We're soft.

speaker: SS
words: The memories of pioneering that you heard and what you knew about— in

speaker: SS
words: cases where the conditions were just rugged, the very beginnings like

speaker: SS
words: that, do you know of what different kinds of effort that demanded of—

speaker: SS
words: let's say the women, the wives in that kind of situation? Would women

speaker: SS
words: do the things that under normal conditions would never have to do to

speaker: SS
words: get by?

speaker: CG
words: They'd get by in any way that they could. It wasn't how do we do it?

speaker: CG
words: It was do we arrive? And they arrived. There were few deaths because

speaker: CG
words: of pioneering. In fact, I don't think I know of any death that was

speaker: CG
